item.title: “Rāhula, develop the meditation on foulness. Rāhula, by developing the meditation on foulness, what is called lust is abandoned. Rāhula, develop the meditation on impermanence.…
item.description:
“Rāhula, develop the meditation on foulness. Rāhula, by developing the meditation on foulness, what is called lust is abandoned. Rāhula, develop the meditation on impermanence. Rāhula, by developing the meditation on impermanence, what is called the conceit ‘I am’ is abandoned.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 62 – Mahārāhulovādasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, just as a forest deer, walking in the forest, walks confidently, stands confidently, sits confidently, lies down confidently. Why is that? Because it has…
item.description:
“Monks, just as a forest deer, walking in the forest, walks confidently, stands confidently, sits confidently, lies down confidently. Why is that? Because it has escaped the hunter’s reach. So too, monks, a monk, secluding himself from sensual desires, secluding himself from unwholesome states, enters and dwells in the first jhāna, which is accompanied by initial and sustained thought, with rapture and pleasure born of seclusion. Monks, that monk is called one who has blinded Māra, who has destroyed his track, who has escaped the sight of Māra.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 26 – Ariyapariyesanāsutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, form is impermanent. What is impermanent is suffering. What is suffering is not-self. What is not-self should be seen as it really is with…
item.description:
“Monks, form is impermanent. What is impermanent is suffering. What is suffering is not-self. What is not-self should be seen as it really is with correct wisdom: ‘This is not mine, this I am not, this is not my self.’ Seeing thus with correct wisdom as it actually is, the mind becomes dispassionate and liberated, not grasping the defilements.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 22.45
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Here, Udāyi, a person lives aiming at the abandoning of attachments, the relinquishment of attachments. But while he lives aiming at the abandoning of attachments,…
item.description:
“Here, Udāyi, a person lives aiming at the abandoning of attachments, the relinquishment of attachments. But while he lives aiming at the abandoning of attachments, the relinquishment of attachments, sometimes, from time to time, distorted thoughts, thoughts and considerations connected with attachments continue to arise. Udāyi, mindfulness arises slowly and he quickly abandons that thought, dispels it, ends it, destroys it. Udāyi, just as a man might drop two or three drops of water onto an iron pan that has been heated all day—slowly those drops fall, quickly they evaporate and vanish. Udāyi, I say: ‘This person is still bound, not freed.’ Why is that? Udāyi, the difference in his faculties has been fully understood by me.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 66 – Laṭukikopamasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “And how, householder, is wandering without a home? Regarding the form element, householder, whatever there is of desire, lust, delight, craving, any engagement and clinging,…
item.description:
“And how, householder, is wandering without a home? Regarding the form element, householder, whatever there is of desire, lust, delight, craving, any engagement and clinging, mental standpoints, attachments, underlying tendencies — the Tathāgata has abandoned them, cut them off at the root, made them like a palm stump, made them non-existent, so that they cannot arise again in the future. Therefore, the Tathāgata is called ‘one who wanders without a home.’ Regarding the feeling element … perception element … formation element … consciousness element — the Tathāgata has abandoned them … Therefore, the Tathāgata is called ‘one who wanders without a home.’”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 22.3
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “And, monks, when that monk dwells in these five things, four more things should be developed. The perception of foulness should be developed to abandon…
item.description:
“And, monks, when that monk dwells in these five things, four more things should be developed. The perception of foulness should be developed to abandon greed; loving-kindness should be developed to abandon ill-will; mindfulness of breathing should be developed to cut off thoughts; the perception of impermanence should be developed to uproot the conceit ‘I am.’ For a monk with the perception of impermanence, monks, the perception of not-self becomes established. For one with the perception of not-self, the conceit ‘I am’ is uprooted, and Nibbāna is attained in this very life.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 9.1
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Stop, thoughts of sensual desire! Stop, thoughts of ill-will! Stop, thoughts of harmfulness! Enough, thoughts of sensual desire! Enough, thoughts of ill-will! Enough, thoughts of…
item.description:
Stop, thoughts of sensual desire!
Stop, thoughts of ill-will!
Stop, thoughts of harmfulness!
Enough, thoughts of sensual desire!
Enough, thoughts of ill-will!
Enough, thoughts of harmfulness!

From: Dīgha Nikāya 17 – Mahāsudassanasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Suppose, monks, a fisherman throws a baited hook into a deep lake. A fish, seeing the bait, swallows the hook. Thus, monks, that fish, having…
item.description:
“Suppose, monks, a fisherman throws a baited hook into a deep lake. A fish, seeing the bait, swallows the hook. Thus, monks, that fish, having swallowed the fisherman’s hook, has fallen into misfortune, into calamity, subject to the fisherman’s will. So too, monks, there are these six hooks in the world, leading to the misfortune of beings, to the calamity of living beings. What six? Monks, there are forms cognizable by the eye that are desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. If a monk delights in those forms, praises them, and dwells with lust for them — then that monk is called one who has swallowed Māra’s hook, has fallen into misfortune, into calamity, subject to the Evil One’s will. There are sounds … odors … tastes … touches … phenomena cognizable by the mind that are desirable … enticing. If a monk delights in those phenomena, praises them, and dwells with lust for them — then that monk is called one who has swallowed Māra’s hook, has fallen into misfortune, into calamity, subject to the Evil One’s will.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 35.230
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: - What gives birth to a person? What always runs about? What undergoes rebirth? What does a person fear? - Craving gives birth to a…
item.description:
- What gives birth to a person?
What always runs about?
What undergoes rebirth?
What does a person fear?
- Craving gives birth to a person,
The mind itself always runs about,
Beings undergo rebirth,
Suffering is what a person fears.

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 1.55
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, what are the defilements that should be abandoned by restraint? Here, a monk, reflecting wisely, lives with the restraint of the eye faculty. Monks,…
item.description:
“Monks, what are the defilements that should be abandoned by restraint? Here, a monk, reflecting wisely, lives with the restraint of the eye faculty. Monks, if he lives without restraining the eye faculty, harmful, feverish defilements might arise. If he restrains the eye faculty, those harmful, feverish defilements are no more. These, monks, are called the defilements that should be abandoned by restraint.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 2 – Sabbāsavasutta
📖 Read this sutta
